title: Inflation Resurgent, Defense Industry Ascendant, and Neo-Ottoman Rhetoric Tests Israel
subhead: May inflation hit 32.6% as the lira slid to a record low, Turkey's defense exports more than tripled since 2021 to $10 billion, and Interior Minister Ciftci's claim that Jerusalem will return to Turkish sovereignty drew a sharp Israeli rebuke.

sections.heading: Inflation Holds at 32.6% as Lira Hits Record Low
sections.body:
Turkey's annual inflation rate came in at 32.6% in May, up from 32.3% the prior month, according to TurkStat data released Friday. Monthly inflation was 1.7%, below April's 4.2% spike but still above market expectations. The Turkish lira dropped below 46 per USD — a new record low — on the release, extending a managed depreciation that has left the currency down roughly 7% year-to-date.

Finance Minister Mehmet Simsek reiterated the government's commitment to disinflation, warning that external shocks would push year-end inflation into the mid-twenties. The central bank last month raised its year-end inflation forecast to 26% from 16%, citing energy price effects from the Iran war.

But economist Mustafa Sonmez, speaking to AGBI, argued that inflation has hovered around 32% or above for the entire past year — not just the three months of the war — and that narrowing demand alone is insufficient when food inflation stays at 35% and housing costs at 45%. 'They cannot make people believe that inflation will come down,' Sonmez said, describing a self-reinforcing cycle of precautionary pricing.
sections.whyItMatters: The Simsek program's disinflation thesis is under strain. War-related energy costs are a real headwind, but the persistence of inflation well above target — across food and housing especially — suggests structural supply-side problems that interest rate policy alone cannot fix. With the lira continuing its managed slide and public inflation expectations unanchored, the central bank faces a credibility gap that grows harder to close.

sections.heading: Q1 GDP Nearly Stalls as Industrial Output Contracts
sections.body:
Turkey's economy came close to stagnation in Q1, with GDP expanding just 0.1% quarter on quarter, according to TurkStat. Year-on-year growth slowed to 2.5% from 3.4% in Q4 2025. The industrial sector contracted 0.8%, while exports of goods and services fell 12% compared with Q1 2025.

Agriculture rebounded by 4.6% on good rains after three years of drought, and the information and communications sector grew 9.5%, but manufacturing — which accounts for nearly 95% of Turkey's exports — bore the brunt of weakening external demand and financing constraints.

The Istanbul Chamber of Industry-S&P PMI offered a modest bright spot, rising from 45.7 in April to 49.8 in May — near the 50-point growth threshold, with new export orders increasing for the first time in 20 months. Economist Serap Durusoy of Abant Izzet Baysal University told AGBI that blaming the war alone would be 'a myopic viewpoint,' pointing to long-term problems of industrial firms accessing finance.
sections.whyItMatters: Near-zero quarterly growth and a 12% export drop signal that Turkey's economy is feeling more than a war shock — structural credit constraints and weakening competitiveness in manufacturing are compounding the external headwind. A PMI near 50 hints at stabilization, but sustained recovery depends on demand conditions Ankara cannot easily control.

sections.heading: Interior Minister Claims Jerusalem for Turkey, Provoking Israel
sections.body:
Interior Minister Mustafa Ciftci declared at an AKP advisory council meeting in Corum on June 6 that Turkey would one day witness the 'liberation' of Jerusalem, comparing it to the fall of Damascus and the Karabakh outcome. He also disclosed a personal prayer from his tenure as governor: 'I prayed to God to grant me the governance of Jerusalem, even for one day.' Lands once under Ottoman rule would 'again come under our sovereignty and dominion,' he stated.

Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz responded sharply: 'Jerusalem is not Constantinople, and the State of Israel is not a crumbling Crusader Empire.' He accused Turkey's leadership of 'working to drag Turkey back into a dark and backward era.'

Ciftci is not a fringe figure — he was appointed interior minister in February 2026 and oversees Turkey's domestic security apparatus. As the Long War Journal analysis notes, his remarks reflect a worldview increasingly common among senior Erdogan officials, blending Islamist politics, Ottoman nostalgia, and Turkish nationalism. Turkey has expanded its military footprint in northern Syria and Iraq, established bases in Qatar and Somalia, and intelligence chief Ibrahim Kalin has repeatedly hosted Hamas representatives in Istanbul.
sections.whyItMatters: A sitting interior minister publicly claiming Jerusalem for future Turkish sovereignty goes beyond standard pro-Palestinian rhetoric — it signals how deeply neo-Ottoman themes have penetrated cabinet-level discourse. Israel's direct condemnation raises the already elevated temperature in bilateral relations, and the episode will be cited by those in Europe and Washington who argue Turkey's strategic reliability is compromised by its ideological posture.

sections.heading: Defense Exports Triple to $10 Billion as Europe Rearms
sections.body:
Turkey's defense exports have more than tripled since 2021 to $10 billion last year, accounting for about 3.7% of total exports, according to a Reuters/Jerusalem Post analysis. Exports to Europe and the US nearly quadrupled to $5.6 billion over the same period. Turkey now supplies about 65% of armed drones used worldwide and is a major ammunition exporter.

Baykar, Turkish Aerospace Industries, Aselsan, and smaller firms like Arca Defense and Kale have moved into markets where Western suppliers face capacity constraints. Indonesia committed to buying 48 Turkish fighter jets currently under development. Baykar acquired Italy's Piaggio Aerospace and partnered with Leonardo on production.

Ankara hopes the NATO summit on July 7-8 — hosting Trump and alliance leaders — will expand arms sales and joint production in Western markets. But Turkey remains largely excluded from the EU's SAFE defense program, and Defense Minister Yasar Guler has urged European allies to abandon arrangements that exclude non-EU NATO members. The collapse of the Franco-German FCAS fighter jet program creates a potential opening for Turkish industrial partnerships, though political barriers around democratic backsliding remain.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's defense industry is a rare economic bright spot and a lever of geopolitical influence. The $10B export figure and 65% drone market share are concrete, not aspirational. But deeper Western integration hinges on resolving political disputes — the EU SAFE exclusion and concerns over democratic governance — that no amount of industrial competence automatically overcomes.

sections.heading: Venezuela's Rodriguez Meets Erdogan as Caracas Seeks New Partners
sections.body:
Venezuelan Acting President Delcy Rodriguez met President Erdogan in Istanbul on Monday for talks covering trade, energy, and mining. Erdogan said Turkey would 'continue to stand by the people of Venezuela' and reaffirmed the shared goal of raising bilateral trade to $3 billion, according to Turkey's Communications Directorate.

The meeting was attended by Vice President Cevdet Yilmaz, Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan, Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar, and Industry Minister Mehmet Fatih Kacir — a full cabinet-level turnout signaling the seriousness Ankara attaches to the relationship. Rodriguez is visiting Turkey at Erdogan's invitation.

The visit comes as Venezuela faces tightened US sanctions and international isolation, making alternative partnerships with middle powers like Turkey strategically valuable for Caracas. Turkey has maintained relations with Maduro's government throughout the Western hemisphere's pressure campaign.
sections.whyItMatters: The full-cabinet attendance signals this is not a courtesy call — Ankara is investing political capital in a sanctions-isolated government. The $3 billion trade target and focus on energy and mining suggest Turkey sees commercial opportunity where Western firms cannot operate. The relationship also reinforces Erdogan's image as a leader willing to defy Western consensus, which plays to his domestic base even as it complicates relations with Washington.

sections.heading: Cyprus: Turkish Cypriot Leader Sets Preconditions for Expanded Talks
sections.body:
Turkish Cypriot leader Tufan Erhurman met UN Secretary-General's personal envoy Maria Angela Holguin on Tuesday and laid out four preconditions for participating in an expanded 5+1 conference: maintaining political equality outside the scope of negotiation, setting clear start and end dates for talks, reaffirming previous convergences, and ensuring no return to the status quo if one side withdraws.

'We will not participate in any process that does not incorporate these four points,' Erhurman said. He described the discussions as 'sincere, useful and productive' but said it was too early to determine where the process leads. An expanded conference involving the Greek and Turkish Cypriot sides plus the three guarantor powers — UK, Greece, and Turkey — could take place in late June, July, or later this summer if preparations are completed.

Erhurman said he did not expect the forthcoming NATO summit to influence Cyprus developments and dismissed suggestions about comments attributed to Erdogan on the issue.
sections.whyItMatters: The preconditions set a firm baseline that could either focus the process or stall it before it starts. The timeline — late June through summer — overlaps with the NATO summit, creating a window where Turkey's strategic attention is divided. Whether the 5+1 format produces substance or becomes another inconclusive round depends on whether both sides see enough in the confidence-building track to justify political risk.

bottomLine: Three threads converged today. Turkey's economy is signaling real stress — inflation stuck above 32%, the lira at record lows, and Q1 GDP nearly flat — undermining the Simsek program's credibility even as the defense industry's $10 billion in exports offers a rare growth story. Interior Minister Ciftci's public claim that Jerusalem will return to Turkish sovereignty marked a new level of neo-Ottoman rhetoric from a cabinet official, drawing a direct Israeli rebuke and reinforcing the tension between Turkey's commercial ambition in the West and its ideological posture. Meanwhile, Venezuela's Rodriguez received a full-cabinet reception in Istanbul and Cyprus talks inched toward an expanded format — both reminders that Turkey continues building partnerships and managing disputes on its own terms, regardless of Western preferences.
sources: AGBI, AP News, Anadolu Agency, Bloomberg, Commerzbank via Europesays, Cyprus Mail, Daily Sabah, Jerusalem Post/Reuters, Long War Journal, The Media Line, Trading Economics, TurkStat
